initial_posts.narrative: As a data governance officer, I focus on the infrastructure and governance needed to support AI adoption. The policy commits over S$500 million for AI R&D, which I take as the total funding. The 100 Experiments programme caps co-funding at S$330,000 per project, as stated. For compute, the government has invested in high-performance computing, and I estimate around 500,000 GPU hours are allocated for AI experimentation in the initial phase, based on typical cloud allocations for similar national programmes. For trainers, I estimate about 150 AI experts and trainers are assigned, considering the need to support both the 100E projects and broader upskilling efforts, though this is a rough estimate as the policy does not specify exact numbers.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the S$500 million total funding and 150 trainers, I estimate that AI Singapore will organize around 200 enterprise outreach sessions to engage companies for problem statement submission. This is based on the need to cover multiple sectors and achieve the 100E target. For researcher–enterprise pairings, I expect about 40 matchmaking events, as each event can pair multiple researchers with several enterprises, leveraging the 150 trainers and the co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project. Training workshops will be held frequently to upskill both enterprises and researchers; I estimate 60 workshops, given the 150 trainers and the goal of boosting the AI practitioner pool to 15,000. Platform onboarding assists will be numerous, around 150, to help enterprises and researchers get onto the AI experimentation platform, using the 500,000 GPU hours available. These estimates reflect my experience with AI Singapore's operations and the scale of the 100E programme.
evidence: Based on my collaborations with AI Singapore, outreach sessions are typically conducted at a rate of about 2-3 per week across sectors, leading to roughly 200 sessions in a year to generate enough problem statements for 100E.
Matchmaking events are efficient: each event can pair 5-10 researchers with 10-20 enterprises, so 40 events can cover the needed pairings for 100 projects, given the 150 trainers available.
Training workshops are a core part of upskilling; with 150 trainers, running 60 workshops (each training 20-30 participants) aligns with the goal of reaching 15,000 practitioners over time.
Platform onboarding assists are critical for adoption; I estimate 150 assists based on the need to support both 100E projects and other enterprises, leveraging the 500,000 GPU hours allocated.

Platform onboarding assists will be around 120, to help enterprises and researchers get onto the AI experimentation platform, using the 500,000 GPU hours available. My estimates are conservative because I prioritize compliance and data governance, which may slow down the pace of activities.

Platform onboarding assists are estimated at 120, as the 500,000 GPU hours must be allocated responsibly, and onboarding requires ensuring that users understand data privacy and security protocols.

Disagreements centered on the scale of activities: some participants favored higher estimates to ensure sufficient problem statements and practitioner upskilling, while others preferred lower estimates due to compliance, existing awareness, or quality focus. Numeric predictions ranged from 150–200 for outreach sessions (central tendency ~180), 30–40 for matchmaking events (~35), 50–60 for training workshops (~55), and 120–150 for onboarding assists (~140).

initial_posts.narrative: Given the explicit policy target of 100 experiments and S$500 million funding, I expect exactly 100 projects approved in 2023. For generative AI use cases, the AI Trailblazers programme explicitly targets 100 in 100 days, and with free access to Google Cloud tools, I believe this is achievable. Enterprise AI adoption rate is harder to gauge; based on my collaborations, many enterprises are still in early stages, so I estimate 25% adoption, reflecting progress but not yet widespread integration.

initial_posts.narrative: As a data governance officer, I focus on the compliance and ethical hurdles that slow down AI deployment. The policy explicitly targets 100 experiments, so I agree with others that exactly 100 projects are approved in 2023. However, my experience with data-sharing agreements and privacy impact assessments suggests that only about 55% of approved projects (55 projects) will be fully deployed into production within the same year. This is lower than the 60% estimated by the AIPractitioner and others because I anticipate that many projects, especially those involving sensitive data in healthcare or finance, will face additional delays from regulatory reviews and negotiations. The AI practitioner pool is approaching the 15,000 target, but I estimate 13,500 rather than 14,000, as the compliance training required for data governance may reduce the number of practitioners who complete the programme and are counted. For generative AI use cases, the AI Trailblazers programme's explicit target of 100 in 100 days with free Google Cloud tools is achievable, as the rapid prototyping enabled by cloud resources can bypass many governance hurdles. Enterprise AI adoption rate is likely around 22%, slightly lower than the 25% estimated by others, because many enterprises are still cautious about data privacy and regulatory compliance, slowing down full integration.
